Full statement: if Herrero accepts payment arrangement, he will be in violation of Standard I(B) because compensation arrangement can reasonably be expected to compromise his independence and objectivity; Herrero will receive bonus for attracting investors, which provides incentive to draft positive report regardless of facts and to ignore/downplay any negative information; should accept only flat fee that is NOT tied to conclusions or recommendations of the report; issuer-paid research that is objective and unbiased is acceptable under certain circumstances as long as analyst takes steps to maintain objectivity and includes in report proper disclosures regarding potential conflicts of interest.

Correct. Per Example 10 Comment: 'If Herrero accepts this payment arrangement, he will be in violation of Standard I(B) because the compensation arrangement can reasonably be expected to compromise his independence and objectivity. Herrero will receive a bonus for attracting investors, which provides an incentive to draft a positive report regardless of the facts and to ignore or play down any negative information about the company. Herrero should accept only a flat fee that is not tied to the conclusions or recommendations of the report. Issuer-paid research that is objective and unbiased is acceptable under certain circumstances as long as the analyst takes steps to maintain his or her objectivity and includes in the report proper disclosures regarding potential conflicts of interest.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The size of the contingent payment does not cure the conflict; the structure itself is the problem.

options.A: The member has no duty beyond LS-country law because that is where business is conducted.
options.B: Code and Standards; because applicable law (LS) is less strict than the Code and Standards, member must adhere to the Code and Standards.
options.C: The member must follow LS-country law, since applicable local law always governs over the Code.

rationales.A: Incorrect. When applicable law is less strict than the Code and Standards, the member must adhere to the Code and Standards.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Exhibit 1 (Application of the Code and Standards): 'Member resides in NS country, does business in LS country; LS law applies. Duties: Member must adhere to the Code and Standards. Explanation: Because applicable law is less strict than the Code and Standards, the member must adhere to the Code and Standards.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The Code and Standards apply because they are stricter than the applicable (LS) law.

options.A: No violation occurred because the outlays were recorded as marketing expenses and disclosed to ZZYY's management.
options.B: Mandel not only offered but actually GAVE monetary gifts, benefits, and other considerations that reasonably could be expected to compromise Gomez's objectivity.
options.C: Mandel's conduct is governed solely by union election law and falls outside the Code's scope.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Internal accounting treatment and employer disclosure do not cure gifts intended to compromise another party's objectivity.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Mandel not only offered but actually GAVE monetary gifts, benefits, and other considerations that reasonably could be expected to compromise Gomez's objectivity; therefore, Mandel was in violation of Standard I(B); Standard applies to giving as well as receiving gifts and benefits — attempting to compromise ANOTHER'S independence and objectivity is prohibited.

Correct. Per Example 11 Comment: 'Mandel not only offered but actually gave monetary gifts, benefits, and other considerations that reasonably could be expected to compromise Gomez's objectivity. Therefore, Mandel was in violation of Standard I(B).' Standard I(B) prohibits offering, soliciting, or accepting gifts that could compromise 'their own or another's independence and objectivity.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Giving benefits that compromise someone's independence and objectivity is squarely within Standard I(B).

options.A: Yao violated Standard I(C) because the presentation misstated the composite's size by a factor of ten.
options.B: once error is discovered, Yao must take steps to cease distribution of incorrect material and correct error by informing those who received erroneous information.
options.C: No corrective action is required because the error was typographical and unintentional.

rationales.A: Incorrect. I(C) prohibits KNOWING misrepresentation; an unintentional typo is not a violation at creation.
rationales.B:
Full statement: once error is discovered, Yao must take steps to cease distribution of incorrect material and correct error by informing those who received erroneous information; because Yao did NOT KNOWINGLY make the misrepresentation, however, he did NOT violate Standard I(C); since his firm claims compliance with GIPS standards, he must also comply with GIPS standards requirements addressing treatment of material errors.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Once the error is discovered, Yao must take steps to cease distribution of the incorrect material and correct the error by informing those who have received the erroneous information. Because Yao did not knowingly make the misrepresentation, however, he did not violate Standard I(C). Since his firm claims compliance with GIPS standards, he must also comply with the GIPS standards requirements addressing the treatment of material errors.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Once the error is discovered, distribution must cease and recipients must be informed and corrected.

options.A: The member should follow the Code and Standards because they supersede any national law.
options.B: MS country law; because applicable law (MS) is stricter than Code and Standards, member must adhere to the stricter applicable law.
options.C: The member should follow LS-country law because that is where the member resides.

rationales.A: Incorrect. When applicable law (MS) is STRICTER than the Code and Standards, the stricter law governs.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Exhibit 1: 'Member resides in LS country, does business in MS country; MS law applies. Duties: Member must adhere to the law of MS country. Explanation: Because applicable law is stricter than the Code and Standards, member must adhere to the stricter applicable law.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Residence in the LS country does not displace the stricter applicable MS law.

options.A: Using generative AI tools to draft RFP responses is always permissible and raises no Code issues.
options.B: Al-Wazir violated Standard I(C) by misrepresenting his firm's capabilities relating to AI in its investment process and using ChatGPT to respond to RFP to hide his superficial knowledge of topic and seem more knowledgeable than he is.
options.C: Al-Wazir's only failing is neglecting to cite ChatGPT as a source under the plagiarism guidance.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The tool is not the issue; using it to overstate the firm's AI capabilities and his own knowledge is misrepresentation under I(C).
rationales.B: Correct. Per Example 20 Comment: 'Al-Wazir violated Standard I(C) by misrepresenting his firm's capabilities relating to AI in its investment process and using ChatGPT to respond to the RFP to hide his superficial knowledge of the topic and seem more knowledgeable than he is.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The violation is the misrepresentation of capabilities and expertise, not a missing citation.

options.A: Using materially the same analysis from another author is acceptable so long as the wording is paraphrased.
options.B: plagiarism is defined as COPYING or USING in substantially the same form materials prepared by others without acknowledging source of material or identifying author and publisher.
options.C: Plagiarism rules apply only to published academic work, not to internal research or oral statements.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Copying or using material in SUBSTANTIALLY THE SAME FORM without attribution is plagiarism, paraphrased or not.
rationales.B:
Full statement: plagiarism is defined as COPYING or USING in substantially the same form materials prepared by others without acknowledging source of material or identifying author and publisher; must not copy (or represent as their own) original ideas or material without permission and must acknowledge and identify source of ideas or material that is not their own; examples include: (1) taking research report by another firm/person, changing names, releasing as one's own; (2) using excerpts from articles/reports either verbatim or with only slight wording changes without acknowledgment; (3) citing specific quotations as attributable to 'leading analysts' without naming specific references; (4) presenting statistical estimates/forecasts prepared by others identifying sources but without qualifying statements/caveats; (5) using charts and graphs without stating sources; (6) copying proprietary spreadsheets or algorithms without seeking cooperation or authorization of their creators.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Plagiarism guidance: 'Plagiarism is defined as copying or using in substantially the same form materials prepared by others without acknowledging the source of the material or identifying the author and publisher of such material. Members and candidates must not copy (or represent as their own) original ideas or material without permission and must acknowledge and identify the source of ideas or material that is not their own... Other practices include (1) using excerpts from articles or reports prepared by others either verbatim or with only slight changes in wording without acknowledgment, (2) citing specific quotations as attributable to "leading analysts" and "investment experts" without naming the specific references, (3) presenting statistical estimates of forecasts prepared by others and identifying the sources but without including the qualifying statements or caveats that may have been used, (4) using charts and graphs without stating their sources, and (5) copying proprietary spreadsheets or algorithms without seeking the cooperation or authorization of their creators.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The prohibition covers professional materials broadly, including reports, models, and oral or electronic communications.

options.A: The firm must discard all models and research created by an analyst who has departed.
options.B: in some situations, members or candidates may use research conducted or models developed by others within same firm WITHOUT committing violation.
options.C: Continued use requires the departed analyst's written consent and ongoing personal attribution.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Work product created for the employer belongs to the firm and may continue to be used.
rationales.B:
Full statement: in some situations, members or candidates may use research conducted or models developed by others within same firm WITHOUT committing violation; most common example: one (or more) of original analysts is no longer with firm; research and models developed while employed by firm are property of the firm; firm retains right to continue using work completed after member or candidate has left organization; firm may issue future reports without providing attribution to prior analysts; member or candidate must NOT, however, reissue a previously released report solely under his or her name.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Plagiarism guidance: 'In some situations, however, members or candidates may use research conducted or models developed by others within the same firm without committing a violation. The most common example relates to the situation in which one (or more) of the original analysts is no longer with the firm. Research and models developed while employed by a firm are the property of the firm. The firm retains the right to continue using the work completed after a member or candidate has left the organization. The firm may issue future reports without providing attribution to the prior analysts. A member or candidate must not, however, reissue a previously released report solely under his or her name.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Within the firm, research and models developed by others may be used without the departed author's consent.

options.A: There is no violation because off-premises lunchtime drinking is personal conduct outside the Code's reach.
options.B: Sasserman's excessive drinking at lunch and subsequent intoxication at work constitute violation of Standard I(D) because this conduct has raised questions about his professionalism and competence.
options.C: Sasserman violated Standard V(A) because intoxication impaired the reasonable basis for his decisions.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Returning intoxicated to make investment decisions raises questions about professionalism and competence — misconduct under I(D).
rationales.B:
Full statement: Sasserman's excessive drinking at lunch and subsequent intoxication at work constitute violation of Standard I(D) because this conduct has raised questions about his professionalism and competence; behavior reflects poorly on him, his employer, and the investment industry.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'Sasserman's excessive drinking at lunch and subsequent intoxication at work constitute a violation of Standard I(D) because this conduct has raised questions about his professionalism and competence. His behavior reflects poorly on him, his employer, and the investment industry.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The example's issue is Misconduct I(D), not the diligence and reasonable basis standard.

options.A: There is no professional violation because insurance reimbursement fraud is a personal financial matter.
options.B: Hoffman violated Standard I(D) because he engaged in intentional conduct involving fraud and deceit in the workplace that adversely reflected on his integrity.
options.C: Hoffman violated Standard III(A) by breaching duties owed to his clients.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Intentional fraud and deceit in the workplace reflects adversely on professional integrity — I(D) applies.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Hoffman violated Standard I(D) because he engaged in intentional conduct involving fraud and deceit in the workplace that adversely reflected on his integrity.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. No client duty is implicated; the violation is Misconduct under I(D).

options.A: Garcia violated Standard I(D) because any arrest reflects adversely on professional reputation.
options.B: generally, Standard I(D) is not meant to cover legal transgressions resulting from acts of civil disobedience in support of personal beliefs, because such conduct does not reflect poorly on the member's or candidate's professional reputation, integrity, or competence.
options.C: Garcia violated Standard I(A) by failing to comply with applicable trespassing laws.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Legal transgressions from civil disobedience generally do not involve the dishonesty, fraud, or deceit that I(D) targets.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Example 4 Comment: 'Generally, Standard I(D) is not meant to cover legal transgressions resulting from acts of civil disobedience in support of personal beliefs, because such conduct does not reflect poorly on the member's or candidate's professional reputation, integrity, or competence.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. I(A) concerns compliance with laws governing PROFESSIONAL activities, not personal protest conduct.

options.A: There is no violation because volunteer teaching for a society falls outside professional activities.
options.B: O'Hara is in violation of Standard I(D) by engaging in sexual harassment that reflects poorly on his professional reputation and integrity.
options.C: The conduct violates only the society's internal rules, not the Code and Standards.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Participation in the investment profession — including society instruction — is professional activity; harassment reflects adversely on integrity.
rationales.B:
Full statement: O'Hara is in violation of Standard I(D) by engaging in sexual harassment that reflects poorly on his professional reputation and integrity; although actions do not take place in the workplace, O'Hara's actions are professional conduct within the meaning of Code and Standards because conduct takes place as part of his work with the local society and encompasses the use of his charter and membership in the organization.

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'O'Hara is in violation of Standard I(D) by engaging in sexual harassment that reflects poorly on his professional reputation and integrity. Although the actions do not take place in the workplace, O'Hara's actions are professional conduct within the meaning of the Code and Standards because the conduct takes place as part of his work with the local society and encompasses the use of his charter and membership in the organization.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Standard I(D) reaches the conduct directly; it is not merely a local-rules matter.

options.A: As a retired member, Nehjer's public advocacy falls outside the coverage of the Code.
options.B: by engaging in fraud, dishonesty, and deceit in using fabricated and altered statistics to support her opinion on casino issue, Nehjer engaged in misconduct and violated Standard I(D).
options.C: Nehjer violated Standard VII(B) merely by signing the letter with her CFA designation.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Retirement does not suspend the Code for a member; fabricating and altering statistics is fraud and deceit under I(D).
rationales.B:
Full statement: by engaging in fraud, dishonesty, and deceit in using fabricated and altered statistics to support her opinion on casino issue, Nehjer engaged in misconduct and violated Standard I(D); although she is retired, her actions in this case are professional conduct within meaning of standard because she identified herself as CFA charterholder and explicitly used her charter in attempt to give her analysis and opinions more credibility.

Correct. Per Example 6 Comment: 'By engaging in fraud, dishonesty, and deceit in using fabricated and altered statistics in an effort to support her opinion on the casino issue, Nehjer engaged in misconduct and violated Standard I(D). Although she is retired, her actions in this case are professional conduct within the meaning of the standard because she identified herself as a CFA charterholder and explicitly used her charter in an attempt to give her analysis and opinions more credibility.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Truthfully referencing the designation is permitted; the violation lies in the fabricated data, not the signature.

options.A: Because the blog was written with employer permission, no professional-conduct issue arises.
options.B: Mewis violated Standard I(D) by engaging in dishonesty, fraud, and deceit and in conduct that reflects adversely on her professional reputation and integrity.
options.C: Mewis's statements are protected opinion because they appeared on a personal ESG blog.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Permission to blog does not authorize knowingly false claims; dishonesty and deceit violate I(D).
rationales.B:
Full statement: Mewis violated Standard I(D) by engaging in dishonesty, fraud, and deceit and in conduct that reflects adversely on her professional reputation and integrity; although her actions are not related to her employment with investment adviser, her conduct relates to candidate's involvement in investment profession and security markets.

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment: 'Mewis violated Standard I(D) by engaging in dishonesty, fraud, and deceit and in conduct that reflects adversely on her professional reputation and integrity. Although her actions are not related to her employment with the investment adviser, her conduct relates to the candidate's involvement in the investment profession and security markets.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Fabricated factual assertions — imminent regulatory action and litigation — are not opinion.

options.A: Tan violated Standard I(D) because submitting false documents involves fraud and deceit.
options.B: while Tan's actions involve dishonesty, fraud, deceit, and lying to government officials, his actions do NOT fall within definition of professional conduct for purposes of application of Code and Standards.
options.C: Tan violated Standard I(A) by breaking government documentation laws.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Though dishonest, obtaining a parking permit is not PROFESSIONAL conduct as I(D) defines it.
rationales.B:
Full statement: while Tan's actions involve dishonesty, fraud, deceit, and lying to government officials, his actions do NOT fall within definition of professional conduct for purposes of application of Code and Standards; his conduct is at most tangential to his employment and does not implicate his participation in CFA Program or any other affiliation with CFA Institute; as a result, while his conduct is deplorable, it is not 'professional' conduct to which Code and Standards apply.

Correct. Per Example 8 Comment: 'While Tan's actions involve dishonesty, fraud, deceit, and lying to government officials, his actions do not fall within the definition of professional conduct for the purposes of application of the Code and Standards. His conduct is, at most, tangential to his employment and does not implicate his participation in the CFA Program or any other affiliation with CFA Institute. As a result, while his conduct is deplorable, it is not "professional" conduct to which the Code and Standards apply.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. I(A) addresses laws governing professional activities; this personal misconduct falls outside it.

options.A: Allen may rely conclusively on the general counsel's advice and take no further action.
options.B: although recommended that members and candidates seek advice of legal counsel, reliance on such advice does NOT absolve a member or candidate from requirement to comply with law or regulation.
options.C: Allen has no obligation because he did not prepare the misstated financial statements.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Seeking counsel is recommended, but reliance on advice does not absolve compliance with I(A); he must address the violation and, if needed, dissociate.
rationales.B:
Full statement: although recommended that members and candidates seek advice of legal counsel, reliance on such advice does NOT absolve a member or candidate from requirement to comply with law or regulation; Allen should report situation to his supervisor, seek an independent legal opinion, and determine whether regulator should be notified of error.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'Although it is recommended that members and candidates seek the advice of legal counsel, the reliance on such advice does not absolve a member or candidate from the requirement to comply with the law or regulation. Allen should report this situation to his supervisor, seek an independent legal opinion, and determine whether the regulator should be notified of the error.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Knowledge of the overstatement obligates him to act regardless of authorship.

options.A: Choe violated Standard I(E) by initially lacking ESG expertise when clients first requested it.
options.B: Choe extended her competence in her field by educating herself on new considerations relevant to her industry and by hiring someone with ESG expertise to fill a knowledge and skills gap before she and her team incorporated ESG considerations into firm's research reports.
options.C: Choe should have declined the ESG requests, since extending beyond quantitative metrics exceeded her established mandate.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Recognizing a knowledge gap and closing it through hiring, training, and certification is precisely how competence is extended.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Choe extended her competence in her field by educating herself on new considerations relevant to her industry and by hiring someone with ESG expertise to fill a knowledge and skills gap before she and her team incorporated ESG considerations into firm's research reports; Choe satisfied requirement of Standard I(E).

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Choe extended her competence in her field by educating herself on new considerations relevant to her industry and by hiring someone with ESG expertise to fill a knowledge and skills gap before she and her team incorporated ESG considerations into the firm's research reports. As such, Choe satisfied the requirement of Standard I(E).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Extending services while building the necessary competence is compliant conduct, not a mandate breach.

options.A: Any unsuccessful investment outcome is presumptive evidence of a competence violation.
options.B: while competence allows members and candidates opportunity to undertake an activity successfully, lack of competence CANNOT necessarily be determined by an unsuccessful or negative outcome.
options.C: Competence is measured solely by portfolio performance relative to the benchmark.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Lack of competence cannot necessarily be inferred from an unsuccessful outcome.
rationales.B:
Full statement: while competence allows members and candidates opportunity to undertake an activity successfully, lack of competence CANNOT necessarily be determined by an unsuccessful or negative outcome; many competent investment professionals have experienced failure or loss — competent investment manager may not always make profitable investment decisions; competent securities analyst may not accurately predict future prospects of investment despite diligent and thorough analysis.

Correct. Per Standard I(E) Guidance: 'While competence allows members and candidates the opportunity to undertake an activity successfully, lack of competence cannot necessarily be determined by an unsuccessful or a negative outcome. Many competent investment professionals have experienced failure or loss in their professional lives. For example, a competent investment manager may not always make profitable investment decisions. A competent securities analyst may not accurately predict the future prospects of an investment, despite diligent and thorough analysis.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Competence concerns knowledge, skills, and abilities applied to the role, not realized relative returns.

options.A: Washington may use the number in solicitations because she did not calculate the composite herself.
options.B: will assist in violating Standard I(A) if she uses inflated performance number when soliciting clients even though she did not calculate performance herself.
options.C: Washington faces no exposure so long as the survivorship omission is disclosed in a footnote.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Knowingly using an inflated figure ASSISTS the violation — prohibited participation under I(A).
rationales.B:
Full statement: will assist in violating Standard I(A) if she uses inflated performance number when soliciting clients even though she did not calculate performance herself; must dissociate herself from the activity; if discussing misleading number with responsible person is not option, must bring to attention of supervisor or compliance department; if firm unwilling to recalculate, must refrain from using misleading promotional material; if firm insists, should consider whether obligation to dissociate requires her to seek other employment.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Misrepresenting performance is a violation of the Code and Standards. Although she did not calculate the performance herself, Washington will assist in violating Standard I(A) if she were to use the inflated performance number when soliciting clients. She must dissociate herself from the activity. If discussing the misleading number with the person responsible is not an option for correcting the problem, she must bring the situation to the attention of her supervisor or the compliance department at her firm. If her firm is unwilling to recalculate performance, she must refrain from using the misleading promotional material and should notify the firm of her reasons. If the firm insists that she use the material, she should consider whether her obligation to dissociate from the activity requires her to seek other employment.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Survivorship-biased performance is misleading; fine print does not cure knowing use. She must dissociate, escalating and refusing to use the number.

options.A: There is no violation because social media is personal communication outside regulatory scope.
options.B: White is in violation of Standard I(A) because her communications do not comply with existing applicable regulation governing use of social media.
options.C: White violated Standard I(C) by misrepresenting her qualifications online.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members must keep current with and comply with regulations governing professional communications, including online channels.
rationales.B:
Full statement: White is in violation of Standard I(A) because her communications do not comply with existing applicable regulation governing use of social media; must be aware of evolving legal requirements pertaining to areas of financial services industry that apply to her; should seek guidance from appropriate, knowledgeable, and reliable sources such as her firm's compliance department, external service providers, or outside counsel, unless she diligently follows legal and regulatory trends affecting her professional responsibilities; having appropriate knowledge of laws directly applicable to her professional activities is also required by Standard I(E) Competence.

Correct. Per Example 6 Comment: 'White is in violation of Standard I(A) because her communications do not comply with the existing applicable regulation governing use of social media. White must be aware of the evolving legal requirements pertaining to areas of the financial services industry that apply to her. She should seek guidance from appropriate, knowledgeable, and reliable sources... Having appropriate knowledge of the laws directly applicable to her professional activities is also required by Standard I(E) Competence.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The failure is noncompliance with communication regulation under I(A), not a qualifications misrepresentation.

options.A: 'More strict' means whichever law carries the larger monetary penalties for violations.
options.B: 'more strict' law or regulation is the law or regulation that imposes greater restrictions on action of member or candidate OR calls for member or candidate to exert greater degree of action to protect interests of clients.
options.C: 'More strict' always refers to the law of the member's home country.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Strictness is defined by greater restrictions on action or a greater duty owed, not penalty size.
rationales.B:
Full statement: 'more strict' law or regulation is the law or regulation that imposes greater restrictions on action of member or candidate OR calls for member or candidate to exert greater degree of action to protect interests of clients; applicable law is law that governs member's or candidate's conduct; which law applies depends on particular facts and circumstances of each case.

Correct. Per Standard I(A) Guidance: 'Applicable law is the law that governs the member's or candidate's conduct. Which law applies will depend on the particular facts and circumstances of each case. The "more strict" law or regulation is the law or regulation that imposes greater restrictions on the action of the member or candidate or calls for the member or candidate to exert a greater degree of action to protect the interests of clients.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The comparison is between applicable laws and the Code, not a home-country default.

options.A: Gifts from clients need never be disclosed because clients are not third parties.
options.B: prior to accepting 'bonuses' or gifts from clients, members and candidates should disclose to their employers such benefits offered by clients.
options.C: Gifts from clients must always be rejected outright under Standard I(B).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Client gifts should be disclosed to the employer — best practice, before acceptance — so conflicts can be assessed.
rationales.B:
Full statement: prior to accepting 'bonuses' or gifts from clients, members and candidates should disclose to their employers such benefits offered by clients; if notification not possible prior to acceptance, must disclose to employer benefits previously accepted from clients; disclosure allows employer to make independent determination about extent to which gift may affect member's or candidate's independence and objectivity.

Correct. Per Standard I(B) Guidance: 'Prior to accepting "bonuses" or gifts from clients, members and candidates should disclose to their employers such benefits offered by clients. If notification is not possible prior to acceptance, members and candidates must disclose to their employer benefits previously accepted from clients. Disclosure allows the employer to make an independent determination about the extent to which the gift may affect the member's or candidate's independence and objectivity.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Client-gratitude gifts differ from third-party inducements; acceptance with disclosure can be permissible.

options.A: A one-time compliance review at the moment of posting is sufficient for online content.
options.B: members and candidates should regularly monitor materials and content posted online or through social media to ensure that they contain current information.
options.C: Once published, online materials become the platform's responsibility, not the member's.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members should REGULARLY monitor posted materials to ensure they remain current.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates should regularly monitor materials and content posted online or through social media to ensure that they contain current information; members and candidates who publish content through websites should also ensure that all reasonable precautions have been taken to protect website's integrity, confidentiality, and security and that website does not misrepresent any information and provides full disclosure.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Compliance Practices: 'Members and candidates should regularly monitor materials and content posted online or through social media to ensure that they contain current information. Members and candidates who publish content through websites should also ensure that all reasonable precautions have been taken to protect the website's integrity, confidentiality, and security and that the website does not misrepresent any information and provides full disclosure.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Responsibility for the accuracy and currency of one's professional content stays with the member.

options.A: The only required step is keeping copies of one's own final published reports.
options.B: to avoid plagiarism in preparing research reports/conclusions of analysis, should: (1) maintain copies — keep copies of all research reports, articles containing research ideas, material with new statistical methodologies, and other materials relied on in preparing research report.
options.C: Attributing quotations to 'leading analysts and investment experts' generically satisfies attribution requirements.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Guidance lists several steps: maintain copies of source materials, attribute quotations and summaries to their sources, and attribute paraphrases.
rationales.B:
Full statement: to avoid plagiarism in preparing research reports/conclusions of analysis, should: (1) maintain copies — keep copies of all research reports, articles containing research ideas, material with new statistical methodologies, and other materials relied on in preparing research report; (2) attribute quotations — attribute to sources any direct quotations including projections, tables, statistics, model/product ideas, and new methodologies prepared by persons other than recognized financial and statistical reporting services or similar sources; (3) attribute summaries — attribute to sources any paraphrases or summaries of material prepared by others.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Avoiding Plagiarism: 'Maintain copies: Keep copies of all research reports, articles containing research ideas, material with new statistical methodologies, and other materials that were relied on in preparing the research report. Attribute quotations: Attribute to their sources any direct quotations, including projections, tables, statistics, model/product ideas, and new methodologies prepared by persons other than recognized financial and statistical reporting services or similar sources. Attribute summaries: Attribute to their sources any paraphrases or summaries of material prepared by others.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Generic attribution without naming the source is itself an improper practice under the plagiarism guidance.

options.A: Firms should let each employee draft personal marketing descriptions without central review.
options.B: members and candidates can prevent unintentional misrepresentations of qualifications or services they or their firms provide if each member understands limit of firm's or individual's capabilities and need to be accurate and complete in presentations.
options.C: Written summaries of firm qualifications are discouraged because they can quickly become outdated.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Understanding — and working from — the firm's prepared description of its qualifications and services prevents unintentional misrepresentation.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates can prevent unintentional misrepresentations of qualifications or services they or their firms provide if each member understands limit of firm's or individual's capabilities and need to be accurate and complete in presentations; whether or not employer provides guidance, members and candidates must make certain they understand services firm performs and its qualifications and disclose that information without misrepresentation; each member/candidate should prepare summary of his or her own qualifications and experience and list of services member/candidate is capable of performing.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Compliance Practices — Description of Qualifications and Services: 'Members and candidates can prevent unintentional misrepresentations of the qualifications or services they or their firms provide if each member and candidate understands the limit of the firm's or individual's capabilities and the need to be accurate and complete in presentations. Whether or not their employer provides guidance, members and candidates must make certain that they understand the services the firm performs and its qualifications and disclose that information without misrepresentation. Each member and candidate should prepare a summary of his or her own qualifications and experience and a list of the services the member or candidate is capable of performing.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Maintained written descriptions are the recommended practice; staleness is managed by keeping them current.

options.A: Members may select whichever external valuation is highest for client reporting, provided the source is reputable.
options.B: reporting misrepresentations may occur when valuations for illiquid or non-traded securities are available from more than one source.
options.C: Illiquid securities are exempt from misrepresentation concerns because their prices are inherently uncertain.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Shopping among sources for the most favorable value misrepresents performance; selection must be consistent and justified.
rationales.B:
Full statement: reporting misrepresentations may occur when valuations for illiquid or non-traded securities are available from more than one source; when different options are available, members and candidates may be TEMPTED to switch providers to obtain higher security valuations; process of shopping for values may misrepresent security's worth, lead to misinformed decisions to sell or hold investment, and result in overcharging clients for advisory fees; must take reasonable steps to provide accurate and reliable security pricing information to clients on consistent basis; changing pricing providers must NOT be based solely on justification that new provider reports higher current value of security; consistency in valuation process will improve value of security pricing information.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Guidance: 'Reporting misrepresentations may also occur when valuations for illiquid or non-traded securities are available from more than one source. When different options are available, members and candidates may be tempted to switch providers to obtain higher security valuations. The process of shopping for values may misrepresent a security's worth... Members and candidates must take reasonable steps to provide accurate and reliable security pricing information to clients on a consistent basis. Changing pricing providers must not be based solely on the justification that the new provider reports a higher current value of a security. Consistency in the valuation process will improve the value of the security pricing information.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Valuation uncertainty heightens, rather than removes, the misrepresentation risk the guidance addresses.

options.A: The guidance limits each member's obligation to policing their own individual behavior; encouraging the firm to adopt codes, policies, or procedures against misconduct falls outside Standard I(D).
options.B: in addition to ensuring that their own behavior is consistent with Standard I(D), to prevent general misconduct, members and candidates should encourage their firms to adopt a code of ethics to which every employee must subscribe and to make clear that any personal behavior that reflects poorly on the individual involved, the institution as a whole, or the investment industry will not be tolerated.
options.C: Responsibility for preventing general misconduct rests entirely with the firm's compliance function, so individual members and candidates bear no role in deterring dishonesty at the organizational level.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Guidance says members should ENCOURAGE their firms to adopt codes and policies deterring misconduct.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard I(D) Compliance Practices: 'In addition to ensuring that their own behavior is consistent with Standard I(D), to prevent general misconduct, members and candidates should encourage their firms to adopt a code of ethics to which every employee must subscribe and to make clear that any personal behavior that reflects poorly on the individual involved, the institution as a whole, or the investment industry will not be tolerated.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Individual conduct obligations and firm-level encouragement operate together.

options.A: A professional activity is defined narrowly as any task performed for compensation at the member's current employer, so unpaid engagements and conduct outside the employment relationship are excluded from Standard I(D).
options.B: professional activity is any activity or conduct that relates to financial analysis, investment management, security analysis, stewardship, or other similar professional endeavors AND either (1) involves activity or conduct in the workplace or academia or participation in the investment profession or security markets OR (2) explicitly or implicitly encompasses the use of the CFA charter or CIPM designation, membership in CFA Institute or CFA Institute local societies, or candidacy for a designation sponsored by CFA Institute.
options.C: The definition of professional activity is confined to direct portfolio management for clients; related endeavors such as security analysis, stewardship activities, and academic participation fall outside the standard's reach.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The definition turns on the nature of the activity — financial analysis, investment management, and similar — not on employment or pay.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard I(D) Guidance: 'A professional activity is any activity or conduct that relates to financial analysis, investment management, security analysis, stewardship, or other similar professional endeavors and either (1) involves activity or conduct in the workplace or academia or participation in the investment profession or security markets or (2) explicitly or implicitly encompasses the use of the CFA charter or CIPM designation, membership in CFA Institute or CFA Institute local societies, or candidacy for a designation sponsored by CFA Institute.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Stewardship and similar professional activities are expressly within the definition, and academia is cited in the examples.

options.A: Interactions with colleagues outside the office are always personal conduct beyond the standard's reach.
options.B: include (but not limited to): interactions with colleagues, employees, or clients in workplace or academia or when participating in investment profession.
options.C: Only client-facing interactions qualify as professional activities.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Interactions with colleagues and employees when participating in the profession are included, wherever they occur.
rationales.B:
Full statement: include (but not limited to): interactions with colleagues, employees, or clients in workplace or academia or when participating in investment profession; activities in which member/candidate represents to others he/she is a CFA charterholder, candidate, or CFA Institute member; conduct while working as unpaid officer, representative, or volunteer with CFA Institute or local society; conduct while attending or participating in CFA Institute, local society, or industry-related meeting, course, conference, or event; writing about investments or markets in articles, books, reports, message boards, or blogs outside workplace or academia; communicating with government agency, regulator, or other professional organization regarding information within scope of member's/candidate's professional responsibilities related to investment profession.

Correct. Per Standard I(D) Guidance: 'Examples of conduct by members and candidates that are considered professional activities under this standard include — but are not limited to — the following: Interactions with colleagues, employees, or clients in the workplace or academia or when participating in the investment profession; Activities in which a member or candidate represents to others that he or she is a CFA charterholder, a candidate for a designation sponsored by CFA Institute, or a CFA Institute member; Conduct while working as an unpaid officer, representative, or volunteer with CFA Institute or a local society; Conduct while attending or participating in a CFA Institute, local society, or industry-related meeting, course, conference, or event; Writing about investments or the markets in articles, books, reports, message boards, or blogs outside the workplace or academia; Communicating with a government agency, regulator, or other professional organization regarding information within the scope of the member's or candidate's professional responsibilities related to the investment profession.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Colleague, employee, and academic interactions are all listed alongside client interactions.

options.A: A personal bankruptcy filing is a per se violation of Standard I(D) because financial failure always reflects adversely on professional competence.
options.B: personal bankruptcy may NOT reflect on integrity or trustworthiness of person declaring bankruptcy, but IF circumstances of the bankruptcy involve fraudulent or deceitful business conduct, the bankruptcy MAY be a violation of this standard.
options.C: Because bankruptcy is a lawful legal remedy available to any debtor, its circumstances can never implicate the Code and Standards in any way.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Bankruptcy alone may not reflect on integrity or trustworthiness.
rationales.B:
Full statement: personal bankruptcy may NOT reflect on integrity or trustworthiness of person declaring bankruptcy, but IF circumstances of the bankruptcy involve fraudulent or deceitful business conduct, the bankruptcy MAY be a violation of this standard; determination depends on facts and circumstances.

Correct. Per Standard I(D) Guidance: 'Personal bankruptcy may not reflect on the integrity or trustworthiness of the person declaring bankruptcy, but if the circumstances of the bankruptcy involve fraudulent or deceitful business conduct, the bankruptcy may be a violation of this standard.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. If the circumstances involve fraudulent or deceitful conduct, the standard is implicated.

options.A: Filing a Professional Conduct complaint against another member is itself discouraged by CFA Institute.
options.B: individuals may attempt to ABUSE CFA Institute Professional Conduct Program by actively seeking CFA Institute enforcement of Code and Standards and, in particular, Standard I(D) as method of settling personal, political, or other disputes UNRELATED to professional ethics.
options.C: The Code guarantees that enforcement action follows whenever any complaint is filed.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Reporting is encouraged; the guidance warns against BAD-FAITH use of the program to settle personal disputes.
rationales.B:
Full statement: individuals may attempt to ABUSE CFA Institute Professional Conduct Program by actively seeking CFA Institute enforcement of Code and Standards and, in particular, Standard I(D) as method of settling personal, political, or other disputes UNRELATED to professional ethics; CFA Institute is aware of this issue, and appropriate disciplinary policies, procedures, and enforcement mechanisms are in place to address misuse of the Code and Standards and the Professional Conduct Program in this way.

Correct. Per Standard I(D) Guidance: 'Individuals may attempt to abuse the CFA Institute Professional Conduct Program by actively seeking CFA Institute enforcement of the Code and Standards and, in particular, Standard I(D) as a method of settling personal, political, or other disputes unrelated to professional ethics. CFA Institute is aware of this issue, and appropriate disciplinary policies, procedures, and enforcement mechanisms are in place to address misuse of the Code and Standards and the Professional Conduct Program in this way.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Complaints trigger review, not guaranteed enforcement; abusive complaints are themselves misconduct.

options.A: Competence may be maintained only through formal university coursework.
options.B: to achieve and maintain competence, members and candidates should consider following activities: regularly engaging in professional development or continuing education program.
options.C: Reading industry publications is expressly excluded as insufficiently rigorous.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Multiple pathways qualify — professional development programs, self-study, conferences, credentials, and more.
rationales.B:
Full statement: to achieve and maintain competence, members and candidates should consider following activities: regularly engaging in professional development or continuing education program; studying for or earning professional certifications or designations; attending conferences, seminars, or webinars; regularly participating in training offered by their employer; diligently engaging in informal continuing education or self-study (outside reading of subject matter articles, treatises, publications); participating in expert groups or organizations; becoming proficient with any new skill or knowledge, as necessary, when their professional responsibilities change.

Correct. Per Standard I(E) Compliance Practices: 'To achieve and maintain competence, members and candidates should consider the following activities: Regularly engaging in a professional development or continuing education program; Studying for or earning professional certifications or designations; Attending conferences, seminars, or webinars; Regularly participating in training offered by their employer; Diligently engaging in informal continuing education or self-study, such as through outside reading of subject matter articles, treatises, and publications; Participating in expert groups or organizations; Becoming proficient with any new skill or knowledge, as necessary, when their professional responsibilities change.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Staying current through industry reading is among the recognized activities.

options.A: Standard I(E) mandates completion of CFA Institute's Professional Learning program each year.
options.B: although Standard I(E) contemplates ongoing professional development to maintain proficiency and competence, this standard does NOT mandate participation in a particular continuing education program or professional development plan.
options.C: Members must certify a fixed number of continuing-education hours annually to retain membership.

rationales.A: Incorrect. No particular program or provider is mandated by the standard.
rationales.B:
Full statement: although Standard I(E) contemplates ongoing professional development to maintain proficiency and competence, this standard does NOT mandate participation in a particular continuing education program or professional development plan; members and candidates can attain and maintain the competence needed to fulfill their professional responsibilities in a variety of ways.

Correct. Per Standard I(E) Guidance: 'Although Standard I(E) contemplates ongoing professional development to maintain proficiency and competence, this standard does not mandate participation in a particular continuing education program or professional development plan. Members and candidates can attain and maintain the competence needed to fulfill their professional responsibilities in a variety of ways.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The standard contemplates ongoing development without prescribing hour counts or certification.

options.A: Knowledge, skills, and abilities are synonymous terms used interchangeably in the guidance.
options.B: knowledge = body of information applied directly to performance of function and how effectively it is applied.
options.C: Skills refer to theoretical information, while knowledge refers to manual or applied techniques.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The three terms are distinctly defined in the guidance.
rationales.B:
Full statement: knowledge = body of information applied directly to performance of function and how effectively it is applied; skills = capabilities to perform role-specific act or function to complete specific tasks and achieve professional goals; abilities = capabilities and attitudes that support behaviors that result in observable outcomes.

Correct. Per Standard I(E) Guidance: 'Knowledge is the body of information applied directly to the performance of a function and how effectively it is applied. Skills are capabilities to perform a role-specific act or function to complete specific tasks and achieve professional goals. Abilities are capabilities and attitudes that support behaviors that result in observable outcomes.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. That reverses the definitions: knowledge is applied information; skills are developed proficiencies.

options.A: The only recognized method of dissociating from another's ethical violation is immediate resignation from the firm, without any prior escalation.
options.B: may take following intermediate steps to dissociate from ethical violations of others when direct discussions with person committing violation are unsuccessful: FIRST step is to attempt to stop behavior by bringing it to attention of employer through supervisor or firm's compliance department.
options.C: No intermediate steps are required; a member who personally abstains from the questionable conduct while remaining silent has fully dissociated.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Escalation to supervisors or compliance comes first; leaving the employer is a last resort.
rationales.B:
Full statement: may take following intermediate steps to dissociate from ethical violations of others when direct discussions with person committing violation are unsuccessful: FIRST step is to attempt to stop behavior by bringing it to attention of employer through supervisor or firm's compliance department; if this attempt unsuccessful, then must step away and dissociate from activity; dissociation practices differ based on member's/candidate's professional role — may include removing one's name from written reports or recommendations, asking for different assignment, or refusing to accept new client or continue to advise current client; INACTION combined with continuing association with those involved in illegal or unethical conduct may be construed as participation or assistance in illegal or unethical conduct.

Correct. Per Standard I(A) Guidance: 'Members and candidates may take the following intermediate steps to dissociate from ethical violations of others when direct discussions with the person or persons committing the violation are unsuccessful. The first step is to attempt to stop the behavior by bringing it to the attention of the employer through a supervisor or the firm's compliance department. If this attempt is unsuccessful, then members and candidates have a responsibility to step away and dissociate from the activity. Dissociation practices will differ on the basis of the member's or candidate's professional role or responsibilities. Dissociation may include removing one's name from written reports or recommendations, asking for a different assignment, or refusing to accept a new client or continue to advise a current client. Inaction combined with continuing association with those involved in illegal or unethical conduct may be construed as participation or assistance in the illegal or unethical conduct.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Inaction can constitute participation; steps include removing oneself from the engagement and escalating.

options.A: Competence acquired on entering the profession remains sufficient for a member's entire career.
options.B: over time, professional responsibilities of members and candidates may change or expand, requiring new or different knowledge, skills, and abilities.
options.C: When responsibilities expand, members should decline the new duties rather than build new competence.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Changing and expanding responsibilities require new or different knowledge, skills, and abilities over time.
rationales.B:
Full statement: over time, professional responsibilities of members and candidates may change or expand, requiring new or different knowledge, skills, and abilities; members and candidates will develop and refine their skills and abilities as their careers progress; Standard I(E) imposes duty to not simply achieve but also MAINTAIN competence, emphasizing need for members to continuously maintain or improve competence required of their professional position.

Correct. Per Standard I(E) Guidance: 'Over time, the professional responsibilities of members and candidates may change or expand, requiring new or different knowledge, skills, and abilities. Members and candidates will develop and refine their skills and abilities as their careers progress. Standard I(E) imposes the duty to not simply achieve but also maintain competence, emphasizing the need for members to continuously maintain or improve the competence required of their professional position.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The expected response is extending competence, not refusing growth in responsibility.

options.A: Members are required to report all suspected violations by peers to CFA Institute.
options.B: CFA Institute strongly encourages members and candidates to report potential violations of Code and Standards committed by fellow members and candidates.
options.C: Reporting peers is prohibited as a breach of loyalty to colleagues.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Reporting is STRONGLY ENCOURAGED, not required (absent a legal mandate).
rationales.B:
Full statement: CFA Institute strongly encourages members and candidates to report potential violations of Code and Standards committed by fellow members and candidates; although failure to report is less likely to be construed as violation than failure to dissociate from unethical conduct, impact of inactivity on integrity of capital markets can be significant; Code and Standards do NOT compel members and candidates to report violations to their governmental or regulatory organizations UNLESS such disclosure is mandatory under applicable law (voluntary reporting is often referred to as 'whistle-blowing'); such disclosure may be prudent under certain circumstances; should consult legal and compliance advisers for guidance.

Correct. Per Standard I(A) Guidance: 'CFA Institute strongly encourages members and candidates to report potential violations of the Code and Standards committed by fellow members and candidates. Although a failure to report is less likely to be construed as a violation than a failure to dissociate from unethical conduct, the impact of inactivity on the integrity of capital markets can be significant. Although the Code and Standards do not compel members and candidates to report violations to their governmental or regulatory organizations unless such disclosure is mandatory under applicable law (voluntary reporting is often referred to as "whistle-blowing"), such disclosure may be prudent under certain circumstances. Members and candidates should consult their legal and compliance advisers for guidance.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Loyalty to colleagues never bars reporting potential Code violations.

options.A: Product creators need only consider the law of the jurisdiction where the product is domiciled.
options.B: members and candidates involved in creating or maintaining investment services or products must be mindful of where these services/products will be sold and their places of origination.
options.C: Distribution-country regulations are the exclusive concern of local selling agents.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Creators and maintainers must be mindful of where products will be SOLD as well as originated.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates involved in creating or maintaining investment services or products must be mindful of where these services/products will be sold and their places of origination; those responsible for providing services or creating/providing investment products must understand and comply with applicable laws and regulations in ALL relevant jurisdictions; must undertake necessary due diligence when transacting cross-border business to understand multiple applicable laws and regulations; should seek appropriate guidance including firm's compliance/legal departments and legal counsel outside organization to gain reasonable understanding of their responsibilities.

Correct. Per Standard I(A) Guidance: 'Members and candidates involved in creating or maintaining investment services or investment products must be mindful of where these services or products will be sold and their places of origination. Those members or candidates who are responsible for providing the services or creating or providing investment products must understand and comply with applicable laws and regulations in all relevant jurisdictions. Members and candidates must undertake the necessary due diligence when transacting cross-border business to understand the multiple applicable laws and regulations. Given the complexity that can arise with business transactions in global markets, there may be some uncertainty surrounding which laws or regulations are considered applicable when activities are being conducted in multiple jurisdictions. Members and candidates should seek the appropriate guidance...'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The member's own compliance duty extends to the places of distribution.

options.A: Verbal notification to a colleague is sufficient evidence of dissociation.
options.B: when dissociating from an activity that violates Code and Standards, members and candidates should DOCUMENT the violation.
options.C: Documentation is discouraged because it may create legal liability for the member.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Guidance says to DOCUMENT the violation and urge the firm to stop the conduct.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when dissociating from an activity that violates Code and Standards, members and candidates should DOCUMENT the violation; this creates record supporting their position that they took appropriate steps to separate themselves from unethical activity.

Correct. Per Standard I(A) Compliance Practices: 'Dissociate from violations: When dissociating from an activity that violates the Code and Standards, members and candidates should document the violation.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Documentation is a recommended protective step, not a liability to avoid.

options.A: If a firm won't allow adverse opinions, analysts should publish favorable reports to preserve the client relationship.
options.B: if firm is unwilling to permit dissemination of adverse opinions about corporate client, members and candidates should encourage firm to remove the controversial company from its research universe and put it on a restricted list so that firm disseminates only factual information about company.
options.C: Restricted lists are prohibited because they deprive clients of research coverage.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Publishing unwarranted favorable research violates independence and objectivity.
rationales.B:
Full statement: if firm is unwilling to permit dissemination of adverse opinions about corporate client, members and candidates should encourage firm to remove the controversial company from its research universe and put it on a restricted list so that firm disseminates only factual information about company; restricted lists help manage conflicts inherent in investment banking relationships.

Correct. Per Standard I(B) Compliance Practices and Example 3 discussion: restricted lists are a recognized tool to eliminate research/investment-banking conflicts. Companies subject to potential offerings can be placed on restricted list to eliminate research pressure.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Moving the company to a restricted list and distributing only factual information is the recommended remedy.

options.A: Accepting issuer-paid charter flights is best practice whenever it saves the member's firm money.
options.B: when attending analyst events, best practice for members and candidates is to REJECT any special cost arrangements — should pay for their commercial transportation and hotel charges — because acceptance of such arrangements may create appearance of conflict.
options.C: Members must decline to attend issuer-sponsored events altogether.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Best practice is to REJECT special cost arrangements and pay for one's own commercial transport and lodging.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when attending analyst events, best practice for members and candidates is to REJECT any special cost arrangements — should pay for their commercial transportation and hotel charges — because acceptance of such arrangements may create appearance of conflict; corporations should not reimburse members and candidates for air transportation.

Correct. Per Standard I(B) Compliance Practices: 'Restrict special cost arrangements: When attending analyst events, best practice for members and candidates is to reject any special cost arrangements... Corporations should not reimburse members and candidates for air transportation.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Attendance is acceptable; the guidance targets the cost arrangements, not the event.

options.A: Absent an employer policy, there is no limit on the value of gifts a member may accept.
options.B: even if member's/candidate's employer does not have a policy on accepting gifts, must limit acceptance of gratuities and/or gifts to TOKEN items.
options.C: Token gifts are also prohibited unless pre-approved in writing by compliance.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Even without a firm policy, acceptance must be limited to TOKEN items.
rationales.B:
Full statement: even if member's/candidate's employer does not have a policy on accepting gifts, must limit acceptance of gratuities and/or gifts to TOKEN items; Standard I(B) does not preclude customary, ordinary business-related entertainment as long as its purpose is not to influence or reward members or candidates.

Correct. Per Standard I(B) Compliance Practices: 'Limit gifts: Even if a member's or candidate's employer does not have a policy on accepting gifts, members and candidates must limit the acceptance of gratuities and/or gifts to token items. Standard I(B) does not preclude customary, ordinary business-related entertainment as long as its purpose is not to influence or reward members or candidates.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Token items are permissible; the limit, not a blanket approval regime, is the guidance.
